name: Saint Louis (Missouri, United States)

colors.symbolism: Spain (red and gold) — the colonial period
Napoleonic and Republican France (blue, white, red); also the United States
the wavy lines of the rivers; Bourbon France (white and gold)
the central golden disc and fleur-de-lis

description: A red field with three wavy white-and-blue arms forming a pall (Y-shape): two arms issue from the upper-hoist and upper-fly corners and a third runs to the lower edge, all meeting at a central gold disc bearing a blue fleur-de-lis. The wavy arms represent the confluence of the Mississippi and Missouri rivers near the city.
history: On 28 November 1962 Mayor Raymond R. Tucker appointed a five-member committee — chaired by Charles Nagel and including Yale University professor Theodore Sizer — to design a new flag for the city's 1964 bicentennial. Sizer's design was officially adopted as the St. Louis municipal flag on 3 February 1964. The colours were chosen to evoke the four flags that have flown over the city: red and gold for Spain, white and gold for Bourbon France, blue-white-red for Napoleonic and Republican France, and red-white-blue for the United States.

features.symbolism: the red field of the flag, recalling the flag of Spain (the city's first European sovereign)

three wavy white-and-blue arms meet at a central point: two issue from the upper-hoist and upper-fly corners, and a third runs to the bottom centre. The branches stand for the Mississippi and Missouri rivers and their confluence near St. Louis
a gold disc at the meeting of the wavy lines, drawing attention to the confluence of the rivers and to the French background of the city's foundation in 1764

features.role: blue fleur-de-lis on the central disc
features.symbolism: fleur-de-lis recalling Saint Louis IX of France, for whom the city is named, and the city's French colonial origin
